The fences that we spent 20 years building to keep bots out are now the things that are keeping our most valuable customers out. When OpenCloud went from a weekend project to 250ome thousand GitHub stars, it became what Jensen called the operating system of the future. The operating system for personal AI.
 Now, everybody's jumped on that bandwagon, right? Nvidia has built an enterprise platform. They've announced on top of it. Everyone's talking about it. But nobody is talking about the structural precondition. Basically, the thing underneath that determines whether any of this actually works. In other words, open claw only works as an idea, as a paradigm if all of our systems are agent readable and agent writable.
 And I don't mean our personal systems. I mean company systems, product systems. I'm not talking about the chatbot here. I'm not really even talking about AI features like whether you can message your bot, your open claw, your claw from some other messaging platform. I'm saying the actual transactional infrastructure, the systems that let someone discover, evaluate and buy or discover, evaluate and use need to be agent readable and writable at core at root as if that's the first and most important use case.
We go back to the idea that Andre Carpath was right that 99% of attention in the future is going to be AI attention. And you know what? It's going to be agent attention specifically. Agents need somewhere to put that attention and actually do something about it. That means readable, writable companies. That's right.
 The entire company has to be agent readable and writable. All of those futures we tweet about where you know something texts you on the WhatsApp and the agent can manage your calendar, can book your flights and this and that. Everyone loves those in demos. All of that depends on a bunch of different companies around the world being agent readable and writable.
 And what no one talks about is that that is a lot of work. That is not easy. And there are a lot of nonobvious implications that we're not talking about because we're so you know, you know why we're not talking about it? We're so hyped up on OpenClaw and the idea of personal productivity. We're forgetting that the entire idea only works if we have a massive, massive company level change.
 an ecosystem level change across thousands of companies that allows agents like openclaw to actually function to their fullest potential. Right now, your open claw, whatever you want to call it, and some people are doing this with claws, some people are doing this with the actual open claw, some people are doing this with perplexity computer, whatever your flavor of choice is, it only works as well as the data the agent can read or write.
 And that's the part we're not talking about enough. Look, I got into this when I was at Prime Video and we were figuring out how to use data to personalize title experiences. And what we discovered is that if you don't have your underlying data clean all the way down the stack, the end experience for the customer is terrible because you cannot provide useful customized experiences without that.
 And that was pre- aent, right? That was just learning how much data matters. Now think about how important it is when agents can actually consume that data at scale. And you need to be able to provide not just visually appealing titles, but the entire range of the product as an agent's consumable interface first. What Open Claw really demonstrated is that people want, they're desperate for, we all want a unified agent that can talk to everything and anything in the digital world on our behalf.
 And honestly, vendors don't want this. Vendors don't want this. and they would not be under pressure to deliver it without Peter Steinberger hacking around all of the walls that we've put up around our products and insisting that we have command line first, chat first agents. WhatsApp is a classic example of something that is not agent readable.
Peter had to do a lot of work to even get it to work in OpenClaw and Meta has spent years figuring out how to lock bots out. But now because Open Claw is so successful, companies are having to rethink that stance because they see the market as moving that way. Everything we learned in the is being flipped on its head right now because we all learned coming up in product that bots were bad because bots polluted real human experiences.
 So you wanted to keep them out. And now all of a sudden it's like no bots are not bad. Bots are how humans interact with the web. Now, it's like when I was growing up, I was told by my parents, "Please don't get into cars with strangers." And now I get into cars with strangers when I take an Uber. Or even more ideally, I get into a car with no driver in it because it's a Whimo.
Things change. Technology forces our behaviors to shift. And we are taking 15 years or more of antibbot architecture and flipping it around and facing the challenge that we now have to build probot architecture. And this is not coming without a battle. Google has quietly apted to shut down bots, OpenClaw bots that are using Google products and services.
 Just a couple days ago, Apple moved to restrict or shut down Vibe coding apps, including Replet in its app store. The big players still have incentive to keep their walls up, but I'm here to tell you that is a losing battle. The people have spoken and the people want agent readable and writable.
 Ironically, Apple profited off of the people speaking about Napster decades ago. The people wanted freely streaming available music everywhere on the web and Napster figured that out. Now, Napster itself got absolutely hammered with lawsuits. But the paradigm of Napster survived and became iTunes. And that's ironically part of how Apple got the iPhone to catch on.
 Now, Apple is facing its own moment with openclaw and responding just like the old guard. Apple doesn't want freely available agent readable writable ecosystems. Google doesn't want freely available agent readable writable ecosystems but the people want it and that is opening the way for a new brand of startups who are coming up and saying we can disrupt incumbents because we recognize where 99% of attention is going.
 Mckenzie projects that by 2030 US BC retail market alone could see up to a trillion dollars in orchestrated revenue from AI agents. That might be conservative. Google, ironically, the same Google, yes, launched the universal commerce protocol to let agents discover products, to let agents build carts, to let agents complete a checkout autonomously.
 Shopify's Toby Lkey has called aentic shopping the transformation of a lifetime. At Shopify, that is definitely going to to be true. And over a million Shopify merchants are coming online to allow agent mediated transactions. So, just in case you think this is all hype and you think whatever, let me give you a specific example that is true and possible today, you can type into your AI assistant, find me running shoes under $120, size 10 that ship before Thursday, from a brand with flexible returns, and you will get a chain of searches and a response with a set of
shoes. the degree to which that response is accurate and useful and the degree to which the agent can then transact that is a function of agent readable writable. And if you want to not have a frustrating shopping experience as a customer, those companies need to get their act together and actually make it useful for an agent to find out that information and then transact on your behalf.
 And this is going to become a matter of survival because more and more shopping is going to happen like that because more and more of our attention is shifting to the chatbot. Nshift's research puts this very bluntly and I think they're right. If delivery windows, if shipping costs and returns are unclear, and I'm not even getting it, the schemas for the product, which also have to be crystal clear.
 If any of that is unclear, the agent is just going to skip over the offer without a human ever even seeing it. and you might have the best product in the world and if it's not agentleible, it's just going to be gone. It's just going to not exist and retailers are going to lose out on that sale.
 And that is going to be something that affects more and more and more of the total share of internet transactions going forward. Now, here's the part that nobody's talking about. If you want to make your entire company agent readable, writable, it imposes enormous changes internally on your data stack.
 You cannot just do it and hope that it will work well. You cannot just do it and not change anything internally and just stick an API on. And why is nobody talking about that? Well, no one's talking about that because we like cheap solutions. We would love the idea that we can just wrap one thing in an MCP server and it used to be an API and now it's agent readable, writable, thank God, and we move on.
 No, it doesn't work like that. You have to actually think through from the ground up what it means to make your product catalog entirely legible to agents. And that has generally been a lot of work. And it's not been worth it when humans were the primary ones interacting with the data. Because humans, we're forgiving. We're generalists.
 We skip details. And we have, as a result, built a lot of systems that are somewhat lazy for customer interaction because we can kind of get away with it. You can't get away with it with agents. In fact, I would argue that a lot of marketing exists to paper over the challenges that come from humans interacting with a product.
 Now, you can disagree with me, but we all know that if a marketer is selling a product with product market fit, everything they do works. And if a marketer is selling a product without product market fit, yet it is running uphill to convince the audience that they should care for the product. that whole function, the whole idea that we need something extra to cover up deficiencies in the product, that's kind of going away now.
 And what we need to start to think about is either a how do we connect amazing products with humans so that humans will love them enough to ask them by name or b how can we start to make agent readable architecture so clean and so clear that we don't have those defects to begin with and no one's trying to complain and say why don't you have this and no one has to make up a reason to think about and care for our product when the product itself is somewhat vague underneath or the data driving the product is not clear.
 Now, you might think, give me an example here. I'll give you an example. Think about the promise of shipping that comes with every Amazon purchase and now increasingly with any purchase across the web. When you go and see that number, that number of days, it is a specific promise for you for that product based on your geography, based on hundreds and hundreds of factors internally that drive the presentation of that experience at Amazon.
 Without that internal data all agreeing, you never get to the point where you could make that promise. Now, Amazon's challenge, which so far they've been resistant to, is to think about how to make that agent readable. How do they make the idea of a shipping promise agent readable when you may want to browse across multiple products and pick partly on the difference in shipping promise? Oh, it's coming today.
 It's coming tomorrow. I have different opinions on what I need. That has not been easy to browse on as an attribute in Amazon in the UI. You have to really dig for it. Well, with agents, it should be really easy. I should be able to say, I want the running shoes that arrive today, and I actually want to specifically have the subset of running shoes that arrive today.
 They're either New Balance or Nike, and I want them because they have particular arch support, and this is the model I need. And the agent should be able to handle all of that in an agent readable manner. And ironically, some of the companies that have the best data for this, and Amazon has fantastic internal data for this, are the ones that are resistant because they are afraid of losing the relationship with the customer.
 That's why these people care. That's why the big companies don't want this because what they don't want you to do is to say, "I'm having a great experience shopping. I kind of don't care if it's Amazon or not. I just want to shop and get the right shoes." That's terrifying if you're a merchant. You lose that relationship with a customer.
 They're not visiting your website anymore. They're just talking with Claude. They're talking with Chad GPT. But guess what? Increasingly, we want to just message the agent and get what we want. We as humans have discovered it's really nice if we don't even have to go to clott JPT and we could just type into a little chat like WhatsApp, hey agent, can you shop for my running shoes that is 250,000 GitHub stars worth of customer validation? And that is what has big companies worried.
 And that is why you should take very seriously the idea that the companies of the future are going to figure out how to orient their data so that it is deeply available to agents. Now, I want to give you two examples that bracket this problem and explain. They're real examples how this works and how it doesn't.
 Stripe is probably the company most people would point to saying there are early adopters. They're getting this right. They figured out the agent economy. And there's a lot to point to that's true about that. But I do want to call out something that I think highlights the product difficulty that we're not talking about. The thing that makes this hard down the stack.
 So, Stripe shipped an MCP server, and that seems great on its own because the agent can look up customers, process refunds, manage subscriptions, things that customers might want to do from the chat. But just shipping an MCP server is not enough. The problem is getting underneath to deeper data is actually really hard.
 So, Stripe has a deeper analytics layer called Sigma. It allows you to query and get full CSVs back with lots and lots and lots of data. There is no limit on the size of the CSV. You're limited by the volume of transactions you have. is functionally unlimited from a query perspective. The problem is simple.
 If you just wrap Sigma, that data source, which worked great as an API, into an MCP, you were going to overload the context window. When you're talking about real business data, it just doesn't work the same way because you have to think about where the data is landing. When you were landing the data as a CSV and it could go anywhere the customer told it to go, save it to local, stick it into a drive, whatever.
That worked. When you are trying to figure out how to load this into a context window natively, it clearly doesn't. In fact, you need an intermediary feature. You need a database. This is the same concept I've talked about as open brain where you fundamentally need the data to flow in. It needs to go into a table structure in a database somewhere and then you need to be able to hit that data from a chat and actually make each agent readable and writable.
 Well, that's a really hard challenge for Stripe because at Stripe scale and with the sensitivity of their data, they have to think about agent security really seriously. You cannot just go out there and knock together a database and say, "Oh, now it works." They have to think very carefully about given their data, what is it about that data that allows them to confidently place a particular view in a particular secure database, ensure correct authentication from the agent and allow the agent to pull slices of that data at will. That is a hard
problem. It is not as simple as just wrapping another API in an MCP. And I think it highlights the complexity even agentleaning companies face when they think about how to make their data more agent readable. And I have sympathy here. I'm not saying you need to make your company agent readable and writable and it will be easy.
 I'm actually saying it's going to be quite hard. It's just that the companies that get it right are going to benefit enormously. SAP is another example. They're on the other side of the spectrum. And I gotta say, they are known for being painful to work with when you want to get data out because the entire incentive for SAP is to keep the data inside.
 Agent readable writable is the future for companies like SAP as well, but they just aren't ready for it. In this case, yes, SAP announced an MCP server for commerce cloud, but the gap between a particular tiniest slice of the overall SAP portfolio has AI features and all SAP installations are agent readable by default is so big you could call it the Grand Canyon.
 If you are running most of the SAP installs out there in the wild, making your systems truly agent readable is a multi-quarter initiative at best. And it's not one right now that SAP has a strong incentive for. It is going to be very interesting in the next 6 to 8 months to see how many companies are going to turn down and look down their stack at their own database structures at their cloud database providers at where their data lives in the stack and say, "Hey, we need to have honest conversations with our vendors about whether the data we have is in a format
agents can read and write against and what it takes to do that." It will be the collective pressure of companies that are seeing where customers are going on their own vendors that are going to push vendors like SAP to be more agent readable and writable. It's going to be one of the most interesting stories of 2026, even if it's one that doesn't make headlines like Open Clock.
Now, I also want to call out four misconceptions. These are real misconceptions I've heard execs talk about in the room with me. And I want to just be very honest and say these are misconceptions. These are wrong ideas about how the agent readable writable future is going to work and I want to be clear about why they're wrong and what we need to do instead because it's easy to say it's going to be agent readable writable and then take the wrong path.
One wrong path is oh it will be agent readable writable but you know we're going to optimize for agent discovery the way we optimized for search. No, no, no, no, no, no. This is a very expensive mistake to make. A search engine returns a ranked list and lets the human choose. And it's influenced by positioning, by brand recognition, by ad creative, by whether you're paying for paid search results.
 An agent doesn't browse a search results page. An agent doesn't browse a list. It evaluates structured data against explicit constraints and then it returns a result. There is no above the fold for an agent. So the companies that win are not going to be the companies that have the biggest ad budgets. They'll have the cleanest schemas and the lowest friction data write and read points, whether those are APIs or MCPS.
 The second big misconception, and this one gets whispered a lot and then it gets said out loud later. The structured schemas that you're talking about, Nate, they only work for simple products. Our business is complicated. Our products are special. They don't really work for that. That's not what our customers would want.
 Look, I hear this from companies that care about luxury, from companies that care about authenticity, from companies that care about how they are positioned to customers as if that's special and unique. This is a fundamental misunderstanding of how agents interact with their world. The more complex your business, the more you benefit from making it agent readable.
It's actually the opposite because that complexity is what prevents customers from optimizing their own purchases today. They often settle for good enough because evaluating all the variables is too complicated. So think creatively and give an agent structured access and allow it to find an optimal answer.
 As an example, I'm a coffee person. Authenticity in coffee is about do you know where the coffee was made? Is it on this little farm? How was it roasted? How was it made? How was it sourced? How was it processed? Those are all schemas an Asian can read from. Yes, you can say authenticity is a vague human, but then you get to the end and what you find is it's a series of data reads and writes, and that's how software works.
 Anything you can represent on a screen is something an agent should be able to read and write against. And that even goes for things like a honeyprocessed Ethiopia yoga chef coffee. I hear this from a lot of people, whether it's a customer saying, "I I'm not going to trust agents to transact. Are you kidding me?" Or it's from a company that says, "I don't think our customers will trust agents to transact because our products are too special.
" Let's look at what's actually happening, not what people are saying. Agent commerce does not start with let the AI buy whatever it wants, which is how people usually frame it. It starts with long horizon intent delegation. Like when I bought a sound system, it starts with hey, I'm considering multiple sound systems. I'm not sure what to purchase.
 My requirement is around, you know, my room configuration. Here's my budget for the sound system. Here's what my old one did that worked well. here's the kind of lossless audio that I want to play, etc. And and it's not like I'm delegating the entire purchase at that moment. I'm actually asking for the agent to have a conversation with me to then think about what is the right call before I go there. So trust is not binary.
 Trust is a spectrum and it starts narrow and then it becomes wider and more permissive as you the individual trust the agent to do more. And that is one of the big stories that people miss when they just treat it as a light switch. Your goal as a company is essentially to enable more of your products to be available across that trust spectrum, which requires the kind of dirty data work that will enable agents to get a true understanding of what you offer and why.
 And by the way, if I've been just showing consumer products and you're you're doing a B2B SAS product, you think this isn't for me, I've got news for you. The consideration funnel is moving into agent world. So agents need to be able to consider your product to see if they should sign up for it. And increasingly, especially in the B2B world, people are going to be asking specifically, can my agent read and interact with your SAS application? So, yes, that is absolutely something you should be paying attention to. And the fourth and most seductive
misconception is simple. We'll just wait and see. This is going to kill companies. I'm just not going to sugarcoat it. The work of cleaning your data is not easy. It takes months. It can take quarters. Getting your data ready to be agent readable is its own project. Let alone making the interface available.
 It takes a lot of deep thought and even companies that are leaning in like I talked about Stripe, they don't have it all figured out yet. If you are waiting and seeing, you are signing your own death warrant as a company because by the time you understand all of the elements of the Asian ecosystem and say we're ready, it will have passed you by and you will not be visible to most of the interactions customers have.
 And if you say no, the world doesn't change that fast. I'm going to remind you the 250,000 GitHub stars for OpenClaw all happened within a few weeks of each other. That's how fast it's changing. Now, this whole thing connects to one of the insights about the agent moment that we're not talking about enough.
 I think because it's hard to put words to it, but it might be the single highest leveraged moment that we have when we think about how to make our data readable and writable because it's so easy to start with this is a basketball. It weighs this much. This is the color. This is the special logo on the basketball and we're done.
 What is hard is to understand how to make the basketball's higher level attributes readable. Let's say the basketball is exactly the same type as the one used in the NBA finals and we care about that if we're basketball fans or maybe it was used in March Madness and we care about the fact that it was used on the court.
It's that it's that exact size, that color, that design, whatever. Think about what happens when a customer interacts with an agent and says, "I want a basketball." You want to be in a position where that higher order intent is something that is also agent readable. when the customer says, you know, I'm watching the game and I want the basketball that is like the one that's on the court with March Madness, the agent needs to take that vague intent and find a real product.
 And if you are not in a position where your data can say, "This is the same ball that was used in the 2026 March Madness tournament, you're going to be in trouble. You're going to be in trouble because that is what the human will ask for." And so really when we talk about vagueness and human intent, part of our challenge as businesses is to figure out what are the common utterances, the kinds of things that a customer might be concerned about with this product that isn't in the traditional metadata.
 And we got away with being lazy about and covering with ads saying, "Get this product because it was in the March Madness 2026 tournament." And instead, we can't make it about ads. We have to actually put it in the data. Well, how do we do that? How do we actually make that a durable data attribute that the agent can read and write against? Now translate that across tens of thousands of products.
 Translate that across the complexity of B2B SAS where you can say you want a product that actually is proven to scale 10,000 customers. And you have to represent that as an attribute that an agent can read and trust that has appropriate sources, not just sources from one case study that you put on your blog. By the way, it has to be dependably shown through technical scaling credentials that yes, this could support 10,000 customers because the agent won't be fooled.
 The agent won't read one blog post and say, "Oh, yeah, that's good enough." Increasingly, the agents we are building are going to be sophisticated enough to take a vague intent human question and go real deep on it and say, "What is the reality here?" And our data needs to be ready for that level of depth. I would argue that so much of this is tribal knowledge.
 It's knowledge that exists in our heads. Maybe it exists in marketing copy and it doesn't exist in our data structures. And so we have the problem of like the opposite of the 8020 problem. We we have the problem where like 20% of our of our data for these products is represented in data structures which in and of itself is challenging to make agent readable and writable.
 Well, 80% of the meaning around the product, the fact that this was a coffee that was processed by a small farmer and is supporting a local school in Ethiopia, all of that, that's in the marketing copy, that's not really in an Asian readable format. And now we have to go and fish it out of a marketing ad or fish it out of the product packaging and stick it into a format that is agent readable.
 That's a lot of work. And that's like 80% of the meaning of the product that the human is going to ask about when they ask about the product. Hey, I want to make sure that my coffee supports important work like building schools. Well, the agent's going to go and look and you're going to see if your product is actually there or not.
 And and you don't want to miss that sale. Look, the entire 2010 software stack was built to keep the agents out, right? Captures are built to keep agents out. Gated APIs are built to keep agents out. Even JavaScript heavy interfaces were built indirectly to keep agents out. All of it was designed around the idea that bots are bad.
 But now the most valuable traffic that your business is going to get over the next three years is bots. AI systems that act on behalf of humans to figure out what they want, to discover products, to compare products, eventually to transact. The companies that make their systems really, really easy to read and to write against are the ones that are going to win this moment.
 And that inevitably means making your data architecture complete even with all of that tribal knowledge that we talked about and making it really clean all the way down the stack. Even if that means talking to your vendors so that you can represent the data in a way an agent can easily digest. And it will not be as simple. I guarantee you it will not be as simple as just taking your existing APIs and wrapping an MCP server.
 That will cover a few percentage points of the use case. That will be helpful. That will be a start. That's lowhanging fruit. It will not be the end of the story as Stripe is discovered. Now, you might say, "How can I make this happen?" Well, I'm going to give you an exercise that will make you sweat a little bit.
 Go to your top three competitors and I want you to actually have a meaningful conversation with Claude or with Chad GPT and I want you to see how far you can get on transacting with them. Test and see what they are doing. Push to see if you can find what agent readability and writability they have. Then do it to your own.
 And I want you to start to benchmark. How long and how hard is it to get data out? Do you have an MCP connector you can just connect in where it's super easy? Is it enough? Is it a start? Do do some of your competitors have that? Is it possible that you could lead in this area if all of your competitors are terrible at this? If they're good at it, is there a way you can rapidly catch up? The companies that are going to win in this world are going to think about agent attention first.
They will structure their businesses and their data around agent attention. And then it turns out, by the way, if you think I'm going to miss the humans, you won't miss the humans because this kind of clean data is actually really easy to build great experiences around for humans, too.
 Did you know that? Like, if you have all of this complex tribal knowledge that lived in marketing copy and now it's in data, it is so easy to power really cool experiences on the web dynamically, personalized for humans. So you could build a personalized landing page about that farmer and that school in Ethiopia for your special coffee and the agent could suggest taking the customer to see that.
 That is easy to do if your data is correct. So build for agents first and the humans will follow. Best of luck.
